description: Registry of historical sex manuals, erotic literature, and erotic ART traditions referenced by the catalog's provenance layer. Each named position attested in one of these works or traditions carries a historical_sources entry linking it to the source, its culture of origin, and the position's historical name where one exists. Sources are typed: 'manual' (instructional/literary texts) and 'art' (visual traditions — sculpture, ceramics, painting, prints). All works and traditions are ancient or early-modern and long in the public domain; the summaries and mappings here are original editorial. Cultural attribution ties named positions to the regions and peoples documented across the wider ethnographic catalog. v2.0.0 adds ten visual-art traditions spanning Mesopotamia, Egypt, Greece, Rome, the Andes, India, Persia, China, Japan, and Renaissance Italy.

sources.title: Kama Sutra
sources.author: Vatsyayana
sources.notes: The most famous of the Sanskrit kama shastra texts; Book 2 catalogues embraces and congress positions with named forms (bandha).

sources.title: Ananga Ranga
sources.author: Kalyanamalla
sources.notes: Late-medieval Indian manual organising positions into classes: uttana (supine), tiryak (side), upavishta (seated), utthita (standing), and vyanta (prone/rear).

sources.title: Su Nü Jing (Classic of the Plain Girl)
sources.author: attributed to the Plain Girl dialogues with the Yellow Emperor
sources.era: c. 2nd century BCE–2nd century CE; preserved in the Japanese Ishimpō (984 CE)
sources.notes: The Nine Ways of the Chinese bedchamber classics — animal-named positions (Dragon, Tiger, Monkey, Cicada, Tortoise, Phoenix, Rabbit, Fish, Crane) — survive through the Ishimpō's medical compilation.

sources.title: Dongxuanzi (Master of the Cavern Mystery)
sources.author: attributed to Li Dongxuan
sources.notes: Tang-era manual enumerating thirty poetic positions — Silkworm Spinning a Cocoon, Mandarin Ducks, Fluttering Butterflies, the Late-Spring Donkey — that shaped East Asian erotic vocabulary.

sources.title: Shijūhatte (the Forty-Eight Techniques)
sources.author: Edo-period pleasure-quarter tradition
sources.notes: The canonical 48 named positions of Edo Japan, styled after sumo technique names and depicted throughout shunga art; modern Japanese vernacular (e.g. ekiben) continues the naming tradition.

sources.title: The Perfumed Garden of Sensual Delight
sources.author: Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Nafzawi
sources.notes: Tunisian manual whose chapter on the 'manners' of congress names positions such as the Stopperage, Frog Fashion, and Driving the Peg Home (Burton's renderings).

sources.title: Ars Amatoria
sources.author: Ovid
sources.notes: Ovid's Book III advises positions by figure, most famously the woman-astride 'horse of Hector' (Andromache's mount) — classical Rome's contribution to the named-position record.

sources.title: Old Babylonian erotic plaques
sources.author: Mesopotamian workshop tradition
sources.notes: Mass-produced fired-clay plaques from Mesopotamian sites — among the world's oldest explicit couple depictions. The signature motif pairs standing rear entry with the woman bending to drink beer through a straw.

sources.title: Turin Erotic Papyrus
sources.author: Ramesside workshop, Deir el-Medina
sources.notes: The best-known erotic artefact of ancient Egypt: a satirical scroll of twelve vignettes showing exaggerated, acrobatic congress — bent-over and standing entries, a lifted-partner carry — millennia before the modern position catalogue.

sources.title: Greek symposium pottery
sources.author: Attic and Corinthian vase painters
sources.notes: Red- and black-figure drinking vessels carried explicit scenes into every symposium, and comedy preserved the vernacular the images illustrate: kubda (the bent-over 'three-obol' position) and keles (the 'racehorse' ride).

sources.title: Pompeian erotic frescoes
sources.author: Campanian wall painters
sources.notes: The lupanar, the Suburban Baths, and private houses of Pompeii preserve Rome's visual position vocabulary. The dominant motif is the woman riding astride — mulier equitans — in both facing and turned (Venus pendula) forms.

sources.title: Moche erotic ceramics
sources.author: Moche potters, northern Peru
sources.notes: The Andes' famous sculptural sex pots: thousands of stirrup-spout vessels modelling couples in the round — rear entry above all, side-lying couples on blankets, and oral scenes — the richest pre-Columbian position record.

sources.title: Khajuraho & Konark temple sculpture
sources.author: Chandela and Eastern Ganga sculptors
sources.notes: The maithuna panels of Khajuraho and the Konark Sun Temple carve the kama shastra tradition in stone — standing carries, raised-leg unions, and acrobatic group compositions on public temple walls.

sources.title: Persian & Mughal erotic miniatures
sources.author: Safavid, Qajar and Mughal court ateliers
sources.notes: Court albums from Persia and Mughal India set couples on carpets and cushions — seated embraces and reclined, legs-raised unions painted with the same refinement as any court portrait.

sources.title: Chinese spring-palace albums
sources.author: Ming and Qing album painters
sources.notes: Painted 'spring pictures' (chungong hua) circulated as albums of garden and chamber scenes, illustrating the classical manuals' positions — chair-seated unions are a recurring signature.

sources.title: Shunga woodblock prints
sources.author: Ukiyo-e masters (Utamaro, Hokusai, Kunisada)
sources.notes: Edo Japan's woodblock erotica — printed by the same masters as the ukiyo-e landscape canon — depicts the 48 techniques in vivid detail and made compositions like the interleaved matsuba famous far beyond Japan.

sources.title: I Modi (The Sixteen Postures)
sources.author: Giulio Romano, engraved by Raimondi; sonnets by Pietro Aretino
sources.notes: Renaissance Europe's notorious position set: sixteen engraved postures suppressed by the Papacy, surviving in fragments and copies with Aretino's sonnets — the West's first printed position catalogue.

sources.title: Etruscan tomb painting, Tarquinia
sources.author: Etruscan tomb painters, Necropolis of Monterozzi
sources.notes: Of roughly 200 painted chamber tombs at Monterozzi, only two carry erotic scenes — the Tomb of the Bulls and the Tomb of the Whipping — deployed in an apotropaic, life-affirming funerary register rather than a narrative one.

sources.title: West Mexican shaft-tomb ceramics
sources.author: Colima, Nayarit and Jalisco potters
sources.notes: The dominant genre is the static joined couple — companionate ancestor pairs encoding marriage and lineage rather than coital position; explicit content skews toward single-figure autoeroticism.

sources.title: 19th-20th century European erotica & early photography
sources.author: Deveria, Fendi, Moulin, Belloc, Rops, von Bayros and anonymous studios
sources.notes: The camera arrives mid-corpus: the position repertoire long rendered in print and paint reappears in daguerreotype and gelatin-silver photography, now entangled with real obscenity prosecutions that used pose and framing, not explicitness alone, as the legal test.

sources.title: Oceanic carving & bark painting
sources.author: Iatmul, Trobriand, Maori and Arnhem Land makers
sources.culture: Papua New Guinean, Maori and Aboriginal Australian
sources.notes: Explicit genital imagery is overwhelmingly instrumental — fertility, genealogy, magical power — with solitary frontal display substituting for the dyadic coital scene of the Eurasian and American traditions. No two-person position depiction is confirmed in this corpus.

sources.title: West & Central African figure sculpture
sources.author: Dogon, Luba and Yoruba sculptors
sources.notes: Paired-figure symbolism dominates: ancestral couples on granary locks, dynastic embraces, Ogboni staff pairs. Nudity marks ancestral or cosmogonic status rather than eroticism, and no depiction of an enacted sexual position is confirmed in the corpus.
